nativeRange.habitat: Zoanthus corals are native to shallow tropical marine environments, where they colonize reef flats, reef crests, lagoon margins, and rubble zones. They are most commonly found in well-lit areas with moderate water movement, ranging from the intertidal zone down to around 20 metres depth.

decorPreferences: stable flat or angled surfaces for encrusting
placed on isolated rock islands to contain spread
spacing from neighboring corals to limit allelopathic encroachment

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental target feeding with small-particle foods — such as Mysis shrimp fines, cyclops, frozen rotifers, Reef Roids, or coral amino acids — tends to accelerate growth and improve coloration. Feeding 1–2 times per week with pumps turned off is a commonly recommended approach.

avoidWith: corals placed in direct contact — Zoanthus encrusts and tends to overgrow neighbors
predatory snails such as sundial snails and box snails (known to prey on polyps)
sea spiders and flatworms that target polyp tissue

imageCandidates.notes: Robert Howie, 5184x3456. Zoanthus sp. under actinic blue reef lighting; neon-blue skirts, green rings and red-orange mouths fluorescing. Foreground polyps tack-sharp, whole colony framed.
Kazvorpal, 1196x933. Classic multicolour dragon-eye morph: orange/green/purple/blue centres with green skirts. Sharp, vibrant, whole colony in frame.
M. Dagnino, 3216x2136. Bright green Zoanthus colony with open tentacle skirts against blue reef water. Sharp, colourful, full colony.
Robert Howie, 3033x2676. Dense colony on dark background; brown/green/orange centres with blue rings and green skirts. Sharp, well-framed.
Franklin Samir Dattein, 2048x1536. Green Zoanthus mat in reef aquarium (clownfish incidental). Shows tightly-spaced button polyps clearly; sharp.
Susan Prior, 1500x1500. Zoanthus gigantus, brilliant green polyps, sharp macro filling frame; single-colour morph variety.
Susan Aide, 2048x1536. Zoanthus colony with blue and green fluorescing polyp centres; sharp, densely packed polyps filling frame.
Ahmed Abdul Rahman, 3607x2702. Zoanthus mantoni in situ; green mat with orange polyp centres. Adds a distinct wild species.
Philippe Bourjon, 1200x875. Zoanthus durbanensis in situ; green-tipped tentacle skirts with bright green mouths. Distinct species; slightly tighter crop.
James Reimer/Angelo Poliseno/Bert Hoek, 699x481. Zoanthus sansibaricus with vivid green centres; smaller resolution, included for species variety.
Nick Hobgood, 1800x2400. Zoanthus pulchellus mat zoanthids in situ on hard coral; muted daylight colour but correct ID, adds a Caribbean species.

summary: Zoanthus corals — commonly called Zoas — are smaller-polyp encrusting zoanthids prized for their vibrant coloration and rapid growth. Hundreds of named color morphs exist in the aquarium trade. Their husbandry is generally considered beginner-friendly, but handling carries a serious palytoxin risk that warrants intermediate classification overall.
careNotes:
SAFETY WARNING — PALYTOXIN: Like their close relatives Palythoa [palythoa-coral], Zoanthus corals are documented to contain palytoxin in some colonies — one of the most potent non-protein toxins known to science. Not every colony carries it in equal amounts, but the risk is real and must be treated seriously.
• Always wear nitrile or latex gloves AND eye protection (splash goggles, not just glasses) when handling Zoanthus, fragging, or performing any maintenance that disturbs the colony.
• NEVER heat, boil, or cook live rock, substrate, or water that may contain Zoanthus fragments. Aerosolized palytoxin has caused documented poisonings, hospitalizations, and multiple reported fatalities among home reefkeepers. Even small amounts of colony tissue heated in water have been sufficient to cause severe respiratory distress.
• Wash hands thoroughly after any handling even if gloves were worn.
• If palytoxin exposure is suspected (skin rash, eye irritation, tingling, respiratory distress, nausea, headache, fever), seek medical attention immediately and inform treating physicians of palytoxin exposure — it is not commonly recognized in emergency medicine.
• See also: [palythoa-coral] — a closely related larger-polyp zoanthid with the same palytoxin risk profile.

Note on care level: Zoanthus husbandry is forgiving in most respects — tolerant water parameters, adaptable to a range of lighting intensities, and fast encrusting growth. The intermediate rating reflects the palytoxin handling risk, which requires deliberate PPE discipline from every keeper regardless of experience level.

Husbandry notes:
• Zoanthus is a genus of 14+ recognized species (including Zoanthus sociatus, Z. sansibaricus, Z. gigantus, and others) commonly discussed collectively in the aquarium trade because all species share near-identical husbandry requirements. Individual morph names are marketing and trade distinctions, not biological species.
• Zoanthus vs. Palythoa: Zoanthus polyps are generally smaller (typically 0.5–1.5 cm across) and more tightly spaced than Palythoa. Both belong to order Zoantharia, but Zoanthus is family Zoanthidae while Palythoa is family Sphenopidae. In practice, both are often sold together under the label 'zoanthids' or 'zoas and palys'.
• Colony growth: Under favorable conditions, Zoanthus tend to encrust and spread rapidly across available rock surfaces, potentially overgrowing neighboring corals. Placing them on isolated rock islands or frag plugs helps contain spread. Plan for ongoing fragging/pruning in established reef tanks.
• Allelopathy: Zoanthus can release chemical compounds into the water that may stress neighboring corals. Regular activated carbon use or a strong protein skimmer is generally advised in mixed-coral systems.
• Lighting: Zoanthus tend to be tolerant of a broad range of lighting intensities (roughly 75–200 PAR), making them forgiving of imperfect positioning. Acclimation from lower to target PAR over 1–2 weeks is still advisable for newly acquired colonies.
• Flow: Moderate indirect flow is generally preferred. Excessive direct laminar flow aimed at the polyps tends to keep them closed. Flow helps deliver food particles and prevents detritus accumulation in the colony mat.
• Pests: Common pests include zoanthid-eating nudibranchs, sundial snails, sea spiders, flatworms, and some crabs. New frags and colonies should be dipped (e.g., Bayer Advanced, CoralRx) before introduction to the display tank. Zoa Pox (white pustule-like lesions on the mat) is a documented bacterial infection that can be treated with Furan-2 or iodine dips.
• Nitrate/phosphate: Unlike SPS corals, Zoanthus tend to tolerate and even benefit from moderate nutrient levels (nitrate 5–20 ppm, phosphate 0.05–0.1 ppm). Ultra-low-nutrient (ULNS) systems may cause color loss or starvation.
• Reproduction: Zoanthus reproduce readily by budding in established reef tanks. Fragging is straightforward and widely practiced. All fraggers must wear full PPE due to palytoxin risk.
breedingNotes: Zoanthus reproduce readily in captivity by budding — new polyps emerge from the encrusting mat (coenenchyme) surrounding existing polyps, gradually expanding the colony footprint. Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs but is not deliberately practiced in home aquaria. Fragging (cutting) with bone snips, a razor blade, or scissors is the standard propagation method among hobbyists; PPE (nitrile gloves and eye protection) is essential during any fragging procedure due to palytoxin risk.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Colony Polyp, Green (Zoanthus sp.)
primary.notes: Primary species page for Zoanthus sp. on LiveAquaria. Provides: temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.0–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, lighting moderate, flow medium, placement middle to top, care level easy, temperament semi-aggressive. Scientific name confirmed as Zoanthus sp.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply — Coral Care Guide: Zoanthids
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 75–78°F (24–26°C), salinity 1.025–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–9.5, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1300–1450 ppm, PAR 75–250. Confirmed beginner care level and moderate flow preference.

additional.name: Reef Chasers — Zoanthids & Palythoa Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C), pH 8.0–8.4, kH 8–9, Ca 400–450 ppm, PAR 150–200 (moderate), flow moderate-high indirect, care level beginner. Palytoxin described as one of the most lethal toxins on the planet; boiling rock explicitly warned against.

additional.name: Reef Builders — Zoanthid & Palythoa Intro Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 75–78°F (24–26°C), salinity 1.025–1.026, kH 8–12, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm. Confirmed palytoxin safety warnings; feeding twice weekly recommended.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens — Zoanthus Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked: PAR 75–125 average (genus-level Zoanthus). Native range: Indopacific (Fiji, Tonga, Solomon Islands, Great Barrier Reef). Palytoxin noted as present in some colonies; becomes a concern when colony is damaged. Confirmed taxonomy: genus Zoanthus, order Zoantharia.

additional.name: World Wide Corals — The Essentials of Zoanthid Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature ~78°F (25.6°C), pH 8.0–8.4, salinity 1.025, flow medium-high indirect, care level easy/beginner. Confirmed palytoxin warning: headaches, fever, hives, and respiratory distress documented from exposure. Never handle above water without gloves.
